Apple nails consistency and realism. Samsung brings sharpness and flexibility. Both have their edge, but it really comes down to what kind of creator you are. Battery and charging. At the end of the day, it's a clash of perfection and power. The iPhone 17 Pro Max is about polish, balance, and seamless integration. So you'll see here, this is the camera itself, and this plugs into an iPhone that's lightning, but we have a USBC version, and I need to order one of those. title: S25 Ultra vs. iPhone 17 Pro Max - Which is Better?
transcript: # Corrected Video Captions I've been using the Samsung S25 Ultra for well  over the past 6 months. The first major difference I've noticed   between these two phones is their weight. And the  S25 Ultra definitely wins this category. It weighs   218 g compared to the 233 g of the iPhone 17 Pro  Max, and I find it just a bit easier to hold and   lift in one hand. Now, that's not to say it's as  light as something like the iPhone Air, which,   if you want to see my thoughts on that, by the  way, and how it compares to the iPhone 17 Pro Max,   make sure you're subscribed to the channel,  and I'll leave a link to the video of that   comparison here once it's up. I don't find myself using that zoom  almost ever compared to the 5x telephoto, which   better crops into the images it produces due to  its higher megapixel count. For nighttime shots,   I've noticed the iPhone has netted me better  results overall than the S25 Ultra. For selfies in good lighting conditions,   they're pretty equivalent between the two  phones. However, in suboptimal conditions,   like when the subject is backlit by a bright sky,  I found the iPhone preserved the shadows on my   face in a way that looked more natural than what  the S25 Ultra did. So, a slight but noticeable   difference there. For the selfie cameras, the  iPhone has a new 18-megapixel Center Stage camera   that automatically zooms out when more people  enter your shot. They made the image sensor a   square size to accomplish this, so you don't have  to rotate your phone for the wider selfie images.   The S25 Ultra has a 12-megapixel selfie camera,  but it doesn't have the new Center Stage feature   or an image sensor that's square in size. For  video quality from the main cameras, it's hard to   notice a difference in quality, but you definitely  notice a difference in overall look. Just like   with photos, the S25 Ultra skews cooler in tone  compared to the iPhone. For the telephoto cameras,   the iPhone did a better job in different  scenes for autofocus, like with these moving   reeds in the foreground and waves crashing in the  background. Though, the iPhone doesn't always get   things right. For example, this video of a rock in  water, the S25 Ultra's color choice was actually   more true to life. Another thing I noticed for  photos is that Samsung's photos tend to be a tad   more sharp, which you can definitely notice when  you peek in at the pixels. They also tend to be a   bit more saturated, especially with the blue hues  that they use for the sky compared to Apple's,   which is a bit more restrained. I also noticed a  few instances where the iPhone actually produced   the better, more contrasty photo. Now, sometimes  it was the S25 Ultra that did that, but more often   than not, in my experience, it's the iPhone, which  was not something I was expecting. Like in this   photo of this tree, which looks less washed  out, or with this photo of this fallen tree,   it was able to get that red hue of the leaves  to look more like they did in real life. Now,   generally, both of these camera systems are pretty  comparable, and the differences are mainly going   to be in the look and tone of the photos that  they create, but overall, based on the photos I've   taken so far, I do have a slight preference for  the photos and videos that have come out of the   iPhone, just because they're often a bit closer  to what I actually see with my eyes in real life.   But if you disagree or agree, let me know in the  comments. It's really close, but I'm gonna give   this one to the iPhone. The next major difference  between these two phones involves their displays.   For years now, Samsung has used an exclusive  type of glass from Corning called Gorilla Armor,   with the S25 Ultra featuring the new Gorilla Armor  2. What makes this glass so great is not only it   reduces reflections like with the original Gorilla  Armor, but Samsung and Corning were able to also   make the display more scratch resistant as well  with the new version. Now, Apple has been trying   to catch up to Samsung's Gorilla Armor for  the past few years with our own co-developed   glass with Corning called Ceramic Shield. And the  iPhone 17 Pro Max has the latest Ceramic Shield 2,   which is improved scratch resistance. And for the  first time, Apple has actually done something to   significantly reduce reflections on the iPhone's  display, though Samsung's display still reduces   them more than Apple's does. All right, next,  let's talk about battery life. Just keep in mind   as I give you my battery life numbers for both  phones that the S25 Ultra has gone through more   charge cycles than the iPhone 17 Pro Max. So,  for the S25 Ultra, in my testing, its battery   life typically reaches somewhere in the 60% range  at the end of the day after a normal day of use,   which for me includes 1 to 2 and 1/2 hours of  screen on time and then 1 to 2 hours of background   activity for music and podcasts. The iPhone 17  Pro Max, so far, I think, has the best battery   life of any phone I've tested. Even on moderate  days with closer to 2.5 hours of screen on time,   its battery percentage at the end of the day has  been in the low 70% range. So, they are close,   but so far the iPhone 17 Pro Max has just slightly  edged out the S25 Ultra. And speaking of charging,   whichever phone you get, when you're juggling  multiple devices most of the time, like I am,   I find it useful to have one charger that can  just handle it all. That's where Anker comes in,   who sponsored this video. Their new Nano  charger comes with 70 watts of power across   three ports. It's 54% smaller than the original  67-watt MacBook charger, yet it packs even more   power with 70 watts of output. The ultrastable,  slim design stays in the wall outlet better than   those bulky chargers that sag or fall out.  With two USB-C ports and one USB-A port,   you can charge up to three devices. Simultaneously  connect a single device to any of the USB-C type   ports to get full 70 watts, enough to power the  iPhone 17 Pro Max from 0 to 50% in 20 minutes or   two MacBooks from 0 to 50% simultaneously within  30 minutes. What really impressed me is how cool   this charger stays during use. Anker's double  gallium nitride technology significantly reduces   heat generation. It's the first TÜV Rheinland  certified 70W lower temperature mini charger. So,   if you need a new charger that you want to be  compact, Anker's got you covered. Get yours   today by using the link in the description below.  All right, next let's talk mic and call quality.   All right, now here's an example of what the mics  sound like just in a studio environment. No case   on the phone. This is what the mics on the S25  Ultra sound like. All right, and this is how the   mics on the iPhone 17 Pro Max sound in a studio  environment with no case on the phone. Let me   know which one you thought sounded better. All  right, and this is the S25 Ultra. This is what   its mics sound like when recorded over a phone  call. I'm recording this on the iPhone 17 Pro   Max. All right, and this is how the iPhone 17 Pro  Max sounds when being recorded over a phone call.   Let me know if you think there's a difference  between them in the comments. For speakers,   it's not even close. The iPhone 17 Pro Max  wins hands down with a more well-rounded sound. It doesn't sound harsh as you increase the  volume, and it has better mids and bass. Now,   we're still talking phone speakers here. So, any  earbuds like the Galaxy Buds 3 Pro that I reviewed   or the AirPods Pro 3 that I'm currently reviewing,  both of these are just going to blow the speakers   out of the water. But if you're going to listen to  your phone speakers a lot, the iPhone definitely   wins. Moving on, when it comes to buttons, the  iPhone 17 Pro Max actually has two additional   physical buttons compared to the S25 Ultra. First  is the action button, which I find very useful.   It's a dedicated button for customizable  actions like turning on do not disturb,   triggering a shortcut, or opening the camera.  Well, until Apple added the camera control button,   which in theory is a button that makes sense. It  opens up the camera and you can use it to control   various parts of the camera, but in reality, it'll  open up the camera when you don't want it to. And   overall, to me, it just hasn't been that useful  outside of just being a shortcut button to open   up the camera. On Android, just double clicking  the power button does this. And while yes,   you can customize the side key action of holding  down the power button to do different things,   it's not as good as having a dedicated button for  customizable actions like the action button. So,   Apple gets credit here. For chip performance,  the iPhone 17 Pro Max's A19 Pro chip bests the   S25 Ultra's Qualcomm Snapdragon 8 Elite chip  by a good margin in single core performance.   However, even though the S25 Ultra has been out  for more than 6 months, in my testing, the S25   Ultra edged out the iPhone slightly in multi-core  performance. For graphics on 3DMark Wildlife   Extreme, the Galaxy S25 Ultra outperformed the  iPhone 17 Pro Max. However, in 3DMark's Solar Bay   Extreme graphics test, which focuses more on ray  tracing and reflections, it was the iPhone 17 Pro   Max that outperformed the S25 Ultra significantly.  Both phones are generally going to be in like the   top 10% of all smartphones for gaming performance,  and some game performance will depend on how well   optimized a game is for each platform. Overall,  I think for chip performance, I'd say it's a tie.   Now, another part of performance is actually with  Wi-Fi and Bluetooth. Both phones have Wi-Fi 7,   the latest Wi-Fi standard currently available.  However, because the iPhone 17 Pro Max got Apple's   own custom in-house N1 chip, that means it got  Bluetooth 6, which does bring under the hood   upgrades that can result in better reliability  with less interference in crowded areas,   lower latency, and enhanced multistream audio  support. All right, so those are all the physical   and performance differences that I've noticed.  Now, let's talk about the differences that stand   out with each phone's operating system as well  as overall ecosystem, which are some of the   biggest ways these phones feel different from one  another. At the time of recording, the S25 Ultra   runs One UI 8, which includes new features like  more on-device processing for some AI features,   more clock customizations on the lock screen,  the integration of Gemini Live into the Gemini   Assistant, and more. While the iPhone 17 Pro Max  shipped with iOS 26, which looks pretty radically   different from the previous versions with the  new liquid glass design, which is this digital   material Apple developed to act like a liquid  in certain instances while always showing the   translucent properties of glass. This version  of iOS also came with some major functionality   improvements like call screening where your phone  will screen unknown calls before ringing your   phone as well as screen unknown texts, and your  iPhone will stay on hold for you now as well,   which has worked quite well in my experience.  In general, in terms of using each phone,   their software is somewhat consistent in terms  of navigation, but the similarities kind of end   there. One UI and the S25 Ultra have quite a few  advantages over iOS, like Gemini as the phone's   assistant, which is slower at playing music and  doing some tasks. Here's "Star" by Jalen Boye on   YouTube Music. But Gemini overall is way more  capable of answering more queries than Siri.   Use peppermint extract, not mint, and add crushed  candy canes near the end of churning for crunchy   pieces. And one thing I love about One UI is the  center swipe for notifications. This is really   useful compared to the iPhone where you have to  move your finger all the way to the top and swipe   down to get to notifications. On iOS, this swiping  down from the center actually brings up Spotlight   search. Good Lock is another advantage of One  UI. This is an app that you can download from   Samsung's app store to further customize your  phone, like creating your own theme with Theme   Park or your own lock screen layout or clock face.  You can further refine the appearance of your   multitasking view, adjust how your notifications  look and are grouped, and add more camera options   to the camera app via the Camera Assistant. One  UI, of course, works well and gets you ecosystem   benefits with other Samsung products as well,  like the Galaxy Buds 3 Pro that I reviewed,   as well as Samsung laptops, tablets, smartwatches,  and appliances. And the S25 Ultra, of course,   also comes with an S Pen, a stylus that allows you  to do things like take notes, handwrite in text   boxes, use it as a magnifying glass, and more. And  if you're a fan of the S Pen and you want Samsung   to keep it in future Ultra phones, let me know  in the comments. And then of course, because One   UI is based on Android, you can sideload apps onto  it, making it, at least here in the US, a bit more   of an open operating system compared to Apple's  locked-down app store, where Apple exerts so much   control over who and what can be distributed  to everyone's iPhone. And speaking of iPhone,   let's talk about iOS's advantages. Probably the  biggest is the Apple ecosystem features you get if   you have other Apple devices. The iPhone is still  kind of the center of Apple's device ecosystem.   By having one, you get messages synced to all of  your devices, the ability to take phone calls on   your Mac, iPad, Apple Watch, etc. You get AirPods  automatic switching, iPhone mirroring on Mac,   which works better than the version you can use  with the S25 Ultra on Windows in my experience.   You get the continuity suite of features,  AirDrop, music handoff, AirPlay. Plus, Apple   just has more class-leading devices than Samsung  like the iPad, Apple Watch, Mac, and AirPods. So,   even though Samsung has many similar features to  the ones I mentioned for Apple, because Apple has   those class-leading devices, I think the pull into  their ecosystem is a bit stronger. Also, while iOS   is more locked down than Android, apps are still  often developed for it first. Take this year's   viral Sora app from OpenAI. When it launched  and for weeks after, it was only available for   iPhone. Or take an app like Notion Mail. Notion  is a really popular service, and right now their   mail app is only available for iOS. Even though  Android phones are more ubiquitous worldwide,   we still have examples of well-known  huge companies developing for iOS first,   which gives the iPhone an advantage. Lastly, let's  talk about price and value. Now, while both phones   offer top-tier performance, camera systems,  displays, and more, there is actually a price   difference between them. The S25 Ultra is actually  $100 more expensive than the iPhone 17 Pro Max,   at least here in the US, though this may not be  the case everywhere. But given the iPhone is more  locked down than Android and feels quite a bit   heavier, but it is $100 cheaper and some apps  are still developed for iOS first in 2025,   which is crazy. The camera system has caught up  to Samsung's. The new selfie camera is excellent,   and the display, battery life, speakers, and  performance are excellent. I think the iPhone 17   Pro Max is the better phone. Even though it feels  like a brick in my pocket, it's less comfortable   to actually use compared to the S25 Ultra, which  really makes me want to give it to the S25 Ultra.   I have to give it to the iPhone this time. So,  those are my thoughts, but let me know if you   think I got it wrong. Am I too obsessed with phone  weight? And which phone you think is better down   in the comments. title: Die besten iPhone 17 Pro Kamera Einstellungen! 📸
transcript: The new iPhone 17 Pro has by far the best camera Apple has ever developed, but only if you set it up correctly.  And I took these impressive photos exclusively with my iPhone in the last few weeks .  In this video, I'll show you step by step my exact best iPhone camera settings, which you can easily adopt.  And I promise you, this will make your iPhone photos look like they were taken with a professional camera.  So definitely stay tuned.  It will be worth it for you .  First, we open the settings on our iPhone and then tap on the Camera option.  There, we select the camera control at the very top .  With the iPhone 17 Pro, you get an additional button that allows you to control the camera .  You can also customize this button and, for example, switch it to code scanner.  When you press the button, the code scanner will open automatically.  Personally, I leave the setting on Camera so that pressing the button immediately starts the camera app and you can take a photo directly with another click.  We now go back one step in the top left corner and make sure that the "Single Click" option is enabled. I then recommend turning off the "Request screen activation" switch, because this is the only way you can start the camera directly even when the screen is locked , without having to activate the screen beforehand.  Under Controls, you then activate Camera Adjustment.  If you press the new button lightly, a menu will open within the camera app where you can customize the camera settings .  Next, we scroll further down to the section on visual intelligence.  Press and hold here to activate the function. Finally, we click on Accessibility at the very bottom .  Here you could completely disable the camera control , but I don't recommend it, as you would otherwise miss out on the previously shown practical functions .  We will therefore leave the camera control activated.  Further down you can also adjust the sensitivity of the camera button .  For example, the speed for double-tapping, if you want the button to respond a little faster or slower.  Once you have made this adjustment, we will go completely back to the camera settings.  With the iPhone 17 Pro, Apple has significantly improved its photographic styles once again .  Tap on photographic styles to adjust and optimize the look of your photos directly in the camera app .  You can set these new styles directly in the camera app itself .  The changes are applied to your photos live, even before you take them.  By swiping left or right, you can easily switch between different moods, and the sliders and grid allow you to adjust the colors and contrast even more precisely.  Back in the iPhone camera settings, we tap cancel in the top left corner, because at the end of the video I'll show you my absolute pro trick.  This allows you to edit your iPhone photos with just a few clicks to make them look truly impressive.  Next up is the best video setting for your iPhone 17 Pro.  4K at 24 frames per second is enabled by default.  You can also switch to 4K at 30 FPS .  This creates a slight motion blur and gives your recordings a natural, fluid look.  I only recommend 4K at 60 FPS if you plan to slow down your videos to 30 FPS later to achieve a subtle time-loop effect.  You can do this directly in the iPhone Photos app by tapping on this icon.  There you can change the playback speed from 100%, i.e. 60 FPS, to 50%, 30 FPS.  If you then play the video, you will immediately see that the middle part is played back much more slowly. If, on the other hand, you want to record a true slow motion , it's best to switch to 120 FPS in 4K.  Personally, I will always stick with 4K 30 FPS.   If you scroll further down in the camera settings , you will find additional options.  Activate the Pahl format if lights are flickering in your videos .  This can happen with certain light sources. Although iPhone stabilization is already very good, slight jerking may occur during fast movements.   That's exactly why you should activate enhanced stabilization to compensate for movement even more smoothly. Also, turn on action mode in low light .  If the message "More lights required" frequently appears in action mode, this slightly reduces stabilization in low light to ensure your recording remains sharp and high-quality. Next, you activate HDR videos to take full advantage of the dynamic range of your recordings.  Then tap on Auto FPS.  By default, the iPhone automatically adjusts the frame rate between 30 and 60 FPS.  However, I deactivate this function so that I can consciously determine the frame rate myself. Back in the settings, you should also activate camera locks so that the iPhone does not automatically switch lenses during recording. Additionally, I recommend locking the white balance to prevent the color temperature from suddenly changing during the movie .  Next, we'll optimize the slow-motion recordings. Tap on "Record in slow motion" to do this.  The iPhone 17 Pro has PH set to 120 FPS by default.  However, you can also switch to 1080 PHD at 240 FPS , which makes the movement appear even slower and smoother .  Personally, I prefer 4K at 120 FPS in Highiciency format, as this gives you the best possible quality with an efficient file size.  Your iPhone also offers Cinema Mode.  Tap on " Record cinema video" to do this. 30 FPS in HD is enabled by default here.  Since movies are usually filmed at 24 FPS, which looks particularly natural to our eyes , I recommend switching to 4K at 24 FPS to achieve the typical cinematic look.  In the next step, we will take care of the sound settings.  To do this, open the "Record audio" option and switch from mono to 3D audio so that you can adjust the sound to be more spatial and precise in post-production .  During editing, you can then choose between Standard, Studio, and Cinema modes in Audix.  For example, we switch to Cinema mode to create a more spatial sound, and the slider also allows you to adjust how strong the cinema effect should be. Finally, confirm your selection with the yellow checkmark in the top right corner. Additionally, enable "Allow audio playback" so that music continues to play in the background during video recording, and turn on " Reduce wind noise" to effectively minimize background noise during outdoor recordings.  Next up are the camera formats.  Tap on Formats.  Here you can choose between high efficiency and maximum compatibility .  High Efficiency mode saves significant storage space while retaining more image information, while maximum compatibility is only useful if you want to edit your photos and videos on your PC as a Windows user without additional conversion .  In photo mode, you now switch from 12 megapixels to 24 megapixels.  I'll show you right after how you can even use the full 48 megapixels.  To do this, activate Prora and resolution control. The item "Prora Format" appears below.  Tap on it and then select Jammer Lostless for maximum resolution. However, I personally use JLossy to reduce the file size without visibly losing quality. After leaving the Prora settings , scroll down and activate the Apple Pro option in the Video Recording section .  HDR is enabled by default there.  Personally, however, I prefer the lock format. Although it may appear flatter and less contrasty at first glance , it offers a significantly greater dynamic range.  After color correction, your photos will appear visibly more detailed, natural, and vibrant. However, you need to connect an external recording device to your iPhone for the lock recording, as the file size in the log is extremely large.  For that, I recommend the external hard drive from Lexa. This can be easily attached to the back of your iPhone using Macve, and as soon as you connect it via USB-C , you can switch from 4K to log format in the camera app and your iPhone will immediately show you how long you can record with the available storage space.  The Lexa ES4 hard drive has another major advantage.  If your iPhone is full again, you can regularly move your photos and videos out of the Photos app and then switch to the files folder .  There you simply select the Lexa hard drive and add your files directly.  The whole process is extremely fast and saves a lot of storage space on your iPhone.  So if you'd like to buy the hard drive , you can find the link to the Lexa ES4 directly in my video description. For most iPhone users, however, Pro is not absolutely necessary, as the standard formats already deliver excellent results.  So we're leaving the camera format settings.  With the iPhone 17 Pro camera, you can also specify that certain settings will be retained the next time you launch the camera app .  To do this, open the "Keep settings" option .  I recommend leaving all options disabled except for camera mode.  This way, you do n't have to switch manually every time you open the camera . Then scroll down and activate Portrait mode as well as the previously set Pr and Resolution Control options. Additionally, you should also activate Apple Pro and Live Photo.  Now, if you open the camera app on your iPhone and Live Photo is active, deactivate it once using the corresponding icon. After that, you can close the Photos app and then reopen it. Live Photo will now remain permanently switched off until you reactivate it yourself .  Back in the camera settings you will find even more useful settings. First, activate "Use Volume Up for Series" .  This allows you to easily start a series of images using the volume up button .  Your iPhone can take up to 10 photos per second, and the longer you hold the button down, the more pictures will be taken.  Directly below you will find two more useful functions.  With QR code scan, you can capture QR codes directly via the camera app without having to open an additional app.  But the "Show recognized text" setting is also extremely useful.  When you photograph a document or sign with text, a symbol appears in the bottom right corner.  If you tap on it, you can immediately copy the recognized text and paste it elsewhere without having to type it manually.  For better image composition, you should definitely activate the grid function.  This divides your image in the camera app into new squares, allowing you to optimally apply the golden ratio and perfectly position subjects in the image. Additionally, activate the spirit level to ensure your photos are always straight. You can safely leave the "Mirror front camera" setting disabled.   It is important, however, to disable ads displayed outside the frame. Otherwise, you'll see areas in the recording that are cut off in the final photo, which often leads to confusion.  iOS 26 also introduced the new category "Ads" .  Here you should activate all the options one after the other so that you can quickly adjust them later directly in the camera app .  With the iPhone 17 Pro, you also have the option to customize your main camera. To do this, open the Fusion Camera option and add additional lenses such as 28 mm and 35 mm.  You can even select one of them as your default camera.  For the best image quality, I recommend the 24 mm main camera.  Ultimately, it depends on your personal taste, of course.  Back in the camera settings, you activate portraits in photo mode.  This allows you to convert a normal photo into a portrait photo afterwards, as the iPhone automatically saves the necessary depth data .  The option to prioritize faster shooting may reduce image quality, so disable it.  Distortion may occur with front-facing or ultra- wide-angle shots .  Therefore, you should definitely turn on lens correction. The macro control, which has been available since the iPhone 14, is of course also included in the iPhone 17 Pro.  It is perfect for very small designs. You set the duration in the bar at the bottom.  For group or family photos, I recommend 10 seconds so that you have enough time to position yourself optimally.  As soon as you press the trigger, the countdown starts automatically.  Since photos taken on the iPhone are a bit too bright by default, you should adjust the exposure, ideally to -0.3 or -0.7. Alternatively, you can tap directly on the screen and swipe down or up using the sun icon to manually change the exposure. Back in the camera menu, you can adjust the aspect ratio of your photo .  4:3 is the default setting. 16:9 is suitable for social media stories, and 1:1 is ideal for square photos like on Instagram.  I generally recommend that you take photos in 4:3 format and crop the image later if necessary .  If you enjoy taking night photos or photographing the starry sky , then activate night mode.  For the best results, you'll want Night Mode Max. Simply press the shutter button and hold your iPhone steady, ideally on a tripod.  After taking the picture, you will immediately see how much clearer and more detailed the image looks compared to without night mode. During the day, you can turn off night mode. title: iPhone 17 Pro Max Street Photography in Bangkok
transcript: [music] Today we are shooting street photography on the brand new iPhone 17 Pro Max. But not much has changed since last year's 16 Pro Max. We've got the same lens and sensor on the ultrawide. We've got the same lens and sensor on the normal camera. But all the big changes this year come to the telephoto lens. title: iPhone 17 vs iPhone 17 Pro 📲 ¿Cuál de los 2 es mejor? #apple #iphone #iphone17 #iphone17pro
transcript: Let's compare the iPhone 17 and 17 Pro to find out which one is better for you to buy. The back of both devices is very different.  The 17 is reminiscent of the previous version, and the 17 Pro has an aluminum body with that glass module in the middle.  It's a complete redesign, and you might prefer one or the other.  What is important to mention is that the 17 Pro has excellent heat dissipation.  The screens are virtually the same: XDR or 120Hz LED. Good viewing angles, colors, and very narrow bezels.  One important thing to note is that both have the boost at 3,000 nits.  They also have an anti-reflective coating and scratch-resistant protection.  Everything is practically the same, even down to the size, which is 6.3 inches.  The cameras are different; specifically, the iPhone 17 Pro has three sensors, the 17 has two sensors, and both have a front-facing camera .  It's really appreciated having this ultra-wide lens on the 17-inch model, and I think it's an excellent option.  I would have liked to see this in the iPhone Air, but the 17 Pro is going to have better and more versatile cameras because it offers more focal ranges.  Both can record at 4K 60 frames per second and 120 on the iPhone 17 Pro; the front cameras are virtually the same.  In terms of performance, we have the A19 Pro in the 17 Pro and the A19 in the 17. And honestly, you wo n't notice the difference here; both perform very well.  The battery is bigger in the Pro, it will give you about three or four more hours depending on your usage. Honestly, considering the specifications and price of the base version of the Series 17, it seems like a better option.

title: Apple's WORST iPhone Case Cost HOW MUCH?! #respect
transcript: iPhone accessory. [music] It's called the leather sleeve, and it launched with the introduction of MagSafe and was only ever made for the iPhone 12 models. The sleeve would provide protection for your phone and use Mag Safe to keep it from falling out. It even had a strap for traveling or wearing as an accessory. [music] There was a tiny sleeve inside the sleeve to store a credit card or ID. At most, it would fit [music] two cards, but then it became very difficult to remove. And my favorite part is how unfunctional these cutouts for the clock and earpiece are. [music] Since the iPhone 12 didn't have an always on display, the clock would quickly go to black. You could see some notifications here, like if you received a call, but you couldn't interact with it. [music] You would have to take the phone out of the sleeve to answer a call.
